# Case study: a public index, safely projected from a private monorepo

**Problem.** The private monorepo is full of things that must never ship: client and employer
identifiers, internal codenames, private remotes, and the occasional secret embedded in a test
fixture. A portfolio still wants to show the *shape* and *scale* of the work. The obvious approach —
copy the repo and strip the sensitive parts — eventually leaks, because copy-then-strip emits
everything by default and depends on catching every bad case forever.

**Design.** This repository is generated by a five-layer, fail-closed pipeline that inverts the
default. (1) An **allowlist extractor** reads only an explicit set of fields from each source item,
so it structurally cannot emit what it never reads. (2) Values are **sanitized before** they reach
the catalog. (3) Anything flagged-but-not-explicitly-aliased is **excluded by default**. (4)
Sensitive **fields are dropped** rather than blanked. (5) A **fail-closed scan** over the final tree
blocks the publish on any known-bad token, and a human approves the irreversible push. Authored and
vendored items stay distinguishable, with license and upstream provenance recorded for everything
curated.

**Why it works.** The safe path is the default path: the extractor's output is small and explicit,
so the later layers are a backstop rather than the primary defense. The whole tree is reproducible
from the private source, so the public repo is a build artifact — never hand-edited, always
regenerable, and diffable when something changes. The final scan reads raw bytes instead of decoded
text, so a binary asset cannot smuggle an ASCII secret past it, and an unreadable file fails the scan
instead of silently passing.

**Trade-offs.** New private content is withheld until someone curates it, so the index can lag the
source — an intentional bias toward silence over leakage. Generic aliases mean some items appear
under sanitized names rather than their real ones. The publisher and its denylist become critical,
well-tested infrastructure. The payoff is a portfolio-grade public index that demonstrates the system
without ever exposing it — and this page is itself the proof.
